Today we are going to have what I think is probably going to be the most fun session. It's going to be copyboarding. It's going to save everybody an absolute shit ton of time. So you'd be crazy. I don't understand if you don't take advantage of what you're going to see today. It's like you're a glutton for punishment or something like that because I'm promising you what you're going to see today is going to save you an absolute ton of time. For what comes next. So I'm going to volunteer soon and I'm going to need somebody with Post-it notes and who can write fast. Who has Post-it notes and can write fast? Anybody? Okay so we're half the battle. Andy's got Post-it notes but he can't write fast. Who can write fast? Okay. Thank you very much. Oh these are good big Post-it notes too. It'll be real fast if I'm fine. Okay I'm giving you, I'm giving these to Matt. Just hold them for a second. And then I'm going to need a volunteer in a second. So think about that, don't raise your hand just yet, but it should be a volunteer whose idea got at least one green light slash I'm in yesterday. So think about that for a second. I'll get back to the volunteer in like five minutes. So what is copyboarding? Copyboarding solves what I believe to be the second biggest flaw in copy. The first biggest flaw in copy is not having the right idea, like we've talked about. There's nothing that's going to fix a bad idea. But even when you have a good idea, the second biggest flaw that I see in copy is that it's not organized the right way. So typically I know this when I open a document and I see that it's more than 70 to 75 pages. Typically when I open a document and I see that it's like page count, 94 pages, I'm like oh shit, like there's something that's going to go wrong in this promotion. Like something that's going to be, you want a tangent that takes this writer into a 30 page, you know, road that he should never be going down or she should never be going down. So that's typically the first sign that the promo is not organized the right way. So what copyboarding does is it helps you solve that second flaw in copy. It helps you organize your promotion in a way that you don't write 95 pages unless you somehow can persuasively write 95 pages. I don't think our business has really ever seen it. But it helps you organize your copies so that you don't get off on tangents so that you know what you have to overcome in what order to make a persuasive sales argument. So the way I typically tell this story is what copyboarding is. I was telling it to my wife one day, it was before Ryan, Doug, myself, Ryan Alarid and Megan D'Amico went down to speak at Todd Brown's event and we were speaking on copyboarding. And I was telling it to my wife the night before and she's like what is copyboarding? And I'm like you know it's kind of like, you know Hollywood would do storyboarding but this is for copywriting. She's like oh I thought it was like waterboarding. I thought you were torturing the copywriter until you got the copy out of it. I'm like no it's a terrible fucking name. It's a terrible fucking name though. We had to rename copyboarding. Anyway, the name stuck. So copyboarding, what it is is kind of like storyboarding, right? Kind of like where if you're going to film a major action movie they're not going to go out there without a script and they're not going to go out there and just say hey just wing it you know for a million dollars on a shoot in a day and we'll do all the stuff in post production. You know we'll edit it all together in post production. They're not going to do that right? Because they're not going to get the story they want to tell. It's going to spend a shit ton of time in post production. What they would do is they would look at the whole story arc that they want to tell and they would begin to do storyboards and they'd say you know what if we moved this love scene before the fight scene? What if we had the fight scene before the love scene? You know what do we have that could be a tension point in our storytelling? So they would do all this stuff and they would move all the pieces of the story around. Then they would go shoot it and it makes their editing much much easier because they're getting the story in the arc that they want it to be in before they spend shit tons of time and shit tons of money and they can just clean it up in post production but not have to create it in post production. It's the exact same thing here. So it bothers my mind that when any copywriter has an idea that they would sit down with a blank piece of paper and just go there it is diarrhea of the mouth. I'm going to write whatever I feel and then it turns into 92 pages that I'm going to give it to somebody to edit. That's crazy to me right? You could save yourself so much time by thinking about your story and writing down effectively the organization of your persuasion before you actually start filling out every single page. So that's what copywriting is going to do. In one piece of paper it's going to hopefully give you from top to bottom the story that you want to tell in the order that you want to tell it. It's going to give you the subheads of your promotion and then from the subheads of your promotion then you can look at that and say from reading top to bottom, from reading from my headline to the bottom subhead does that tell the story that I wanted to tell just in the subheads. So that is kind of a big trick right? You want to be able to tell your entire persuasive story just by reading your subheads. Then if you get to the point at the end of the day using the copywriting process that your subheads tell the story all you therefore do in the next couple days of your life of writing a promotion is you go back in and you fill in between your subheads what you need that subhead to prove. And again this all makes sense kind of in a couple seconds. So hold that thought for just a moment. So when you look at the sales letter right? A sales letter to me is nothing but a series of mini-sells. So you're selling the reader on a lot of different mini-items that will persuasively cause them to act at the end of the sales letter. So let's look at the headline. What does the headline have to sell? The headline has to sell you on getting your attention. That is the role of the headline. That's a mini-sell. After you get attention somewhere in the process you're going to have to make another sell. We talked about this sell. What is that sell? After you've gained attention. Close. You're going to have to sell a promise somewhere in there. But there's one specific one I'm looking for too. You have to sell proof, right? You have to sell that they're going to believe in this strategy. So when you think about a sales letter you know again it's going to be one big idea but it's a series of mini-sells that you have to tackle in order that the customer is going to face that objection and need to be sold on that idea. The worst thing that you can do after you have a big idea and after you begin to organize your promotion is that you can, the worst thing you can do is not overcome an objection that the reader is feeling. Because if the reader has an objection in the middle of that body copy and you're not overcoming it somewhat soon they're going to be frustrated and they're going to leave. They cannot believe you or they can be frustrated by not feeling that this is a unique enough mechanism. So when you organize your sales you're going to organize them by anticipating the objection that the reader is going to face and trying to overcome that objection by reselling them into that promotion. So again this is all going to seem complicated. We'll make it very, very, very, very easy in just a moment. So when you look at copyboarding step number one is of course you need a big idea. Again I cannot help you. This will not help you unless you have a big idea. Step number two when you look at copyboarding is that you're going to take out post-it notes and we'll demonstrate this in a second and you're going to write down every objection that you think that reader will feel in your promotion. During this first process, first part of the process, you're just brainstorming objections. You don't have to do them in any order. So what we'll do as a group is we'll pick somebody that volunteer in a second, we'll have them restate the pitch that they had yesterday and then we'll begin brainstorming objections as a group that we think the reader is going to face when you hear that idea. Again at that point you're not trying to organize anything. So what we're going to do here in a second when Matt writes on Andy's post-it notes is we're just going to throw kind of post-it notes all up over here with objections. So step number one is you need your idea. Step number two is that you're going to brainstorm all the objections that you believe the reader would be facing. Step number three is that you're going to begin to organize those objections. You know what objection is the reader going to feel first? What objection do we have to overcome first? What do we have to sell the reader on first after we've gotten their attention? When you're doing this, in the best way possible, you're probably going to organize your post-it notes from top to low, which is going to be your flow of your promotion. So that's step number three is that you're going to be reorganizing these very unorganized post-it notes in order of the objections from top to low that you're going to have to overcome for the reader. Step number four is beginning the selling process. You're going to have to take that post-it note and rewrite that post-it note into a subhead that sells the reader by overcoming that objection. And we'll get to that in a second. And then step number five is what you're going to be doing during Hell Week, which is at that point you should have all of your objections in order that you think the reader is going to face them. You should have turned those into mini-cells with subheads. And step number five is that you fill in in between the subheads. So what this is going to allow you to do is you're going to see problems in the persuasion because you're going to look at all of these post-it notes kind of linear, right? And you're going to see all these cells linear. And if you can't read from your headline down to each one of those mini-subheads and not make the case, then something's wrong with your sales letter. And you're going to see that before you ever begin to fill in in between these subheads. This is going to be the easiest spot to figure out if something is wrong with your sales letter. And I promise you, if you just start writing, you're not going to, because you're going to write with a blank piece of paper, you're going to have that flaw that everybody has, which is it's really tough to make a sales argument over the course of 30 or 40 pages. If you don't have an outline of what you want to say and you're doing it over the courses of multiple days or your emotions changing and you're starting and you're stopping, that's where you get to the point of you turning copies. It's like 75 pages because it's not organized. You haven't thought about the structure. And it becomes much, much, much harder to edit at that point when you haven't done the copyboarding process because it's commitment and consistency again. When you write 75 pages, you're going to feel committed to all 75 of those pages. It's going to be much harder to say those 25 pages don't belong anymore because they shouldn't have belonged in the first place. But you're going to feel that they belong because you're going to have sold on yourself when you've done all this work. You don't want to delete these things. So doing this will save you an absolute ton of time. I don't know why anyone wouldn't do this. I still see copy being turned in and I can tell for certain that the person has not went through this process as much as I believe it could benefit them. So I think if you try it just once and you try it on your next promotion, you'll never not do it again. But it's got to be trying it on that first promotion first. So my guess is that you'll be sold after the first time you do it. I always turn it over to Pete and Matt because the three of us have worked on this a couple times. I think it's benefited them but I think you guys should hear it from them versus me. So has it been helpful? Either one of you guys can start. Yeah, I'd say everything Joe said is gospel truth. So I don't listen to it. I don't ask. And I know Matt did RGS obviously which hit over $10 million, well like $11 million, $12 million. And since, I guess we started with this in November 2015. And since then, I think I've done like 14 or 15 all together on the backend using this and one form or another. You're either perfect or you're perfect. And I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. And I think that's the best way to do it. And I think that's the best way to do it. And I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. I think that's the best way to do it. Let me say another thing. This is going to be a hyper accelerated example. This process should take 2 hours. This is going to take 2 hours. Why haven't I heard about this before? What do you need? Sorry, go for it. I think we could just be rapid firing. I'm trying to have 2 people writing. I think I'm going to have to do it. I think I'm going to have to do it. This is a side effect. Does this mean I'm never going to die? Hit me. How does it work? Is this safe? How did you find this? This one's been around for a few years. I'm going to do Y now. This is where we prove that this is not a perfect process. How much is the actual thing? Do you have to be a certain age? Is this sort of thing regulated? Is that the same as what age drives me? Hit me with one. Where can I get some? About that. Am I too old? Yes, Ryan. How long do you think this is? How long? How long? How am I going to be able to tell that this is working? Let's take three more. Is it something I can get regularly? Is it covered by Medicare? I'm going to do Medicare questions. This is step number two. Signorelli's got his work to do there. That's what your sales knit letter needs to do. This is why it's super dangerous to begin writing without an organization. His chances are that if Signorelli starts writing without it being organized, he's not going to cover some of those objections. He's going to cover them in the wrong order. This is why it's critical that you anticipate those objections, which is going to come next and overcome that objection before the reader has to face it themselves. If you do not overcome an objection that the reader feels, they're going to get frustrated and it's going to be one of the points that they leave. Maybe they're confused in that way that they believe. Maybe they find it unbelievable because you're not overcoming the objection of, why should I believe you? Maybe they find it boring because you're not pushing the sale forward. This next step is going to be critical. We're moving on to step number three, which is now we have all of these objections. We have to try to anticipate in the order that the reader is going to face those objections. What is that order? So now what Minsley is going to do? That's about to make my escape. Actually, you know what? Minsley, sit down. Yes, yes. Who's the tallest person in this room? You see that? It's probably Andy or Doug, right? Andy, stand up. Who's got it? Look at Doug shinking down. Doug, you mind? You have to go to the volunteering blindly. Doug is the tallest person in the room. He's going to be getting organized from the very, very tippy top of that ceiling. Then we're going to switch over to the shortest person in the room that probably is me. I'm going to ask for a different short volunteer. It's going to organize everything down by the bottom of the floor. So again, this is where it's going to get imperfect because there's 20 different opinions here. It should just be a smaller group, but to demonstrate it, even though it's going to be as perfect, call out, someone's going to commit. What do you think after hearing that idea that Jim just pitched? I'm going to ask him to pitch it again in just a second. We're going to try to organize these from top to bottom, right? Doug is going to pick the post-it note that someone thinks is the largest objection that someone's going to feel. The first objection they feel after hearing that idea. None is going to put that thing all the way at the top of the room. Then he's going to organize it from top to low. This is going to be like what you see in a Word document, right? From top to bottom. So, Signorelli, pitch that idea one more time. Scientists have discovered a protein that causes aging. Pharmacompany are trying to come up with drugs that inhibit this protein. There's a natural compound that actually does it, that already exists. It comes from the milk thistle plant, which folklore says comes from the leaves of it. The white fluid comes from the milk of the Virgin Mary. A side benefit in doing this is how many times have you pitched that idea now? From the first time you mentioned in the copycam to right now? Probably about four. Four times, right? So he's constantly pitching this idea, which is a side kind of benefit of doing this. He's constantly refining this pitch. That pitch is getting tighter and tighter, as you can see too. I mean, you just pitched the same idea that you pitched yesterday, that you pitched in half the time too, because you're refining that pitch. Okay, so with that pitch, again, somebody's going to have to commit to it, and we're not really going to argue too much as it would be, because it's going to take forever. So for demonstration purposes only, someone commit to, what's the first post-it note that's up there, after hearing that idea that you think Jim's going to have to overcome? Why haven't I heard about this? Oh, wait, sorry. I'm going to put it up first. Why haven't I heard about this? Okay, so can you find that post-it note? Why haven't I heard about this? I can't read any of these. You need to come up there and just type it in. There we go. Okay, put that all the way at the top. Okay, why haven't I heard about this? And then double. Can you do me another favor? Sure. Who's got the post-it notes? Can you put a blank post-it note beside that? Yep. I need point one? Yes. Thank you. Okay, next one. So let's assume we're overcoming that. What does it have to do with Mary? What does it have to do with Mary? Middle. It's there. All right. It's not going to be us. I'm going to put it here. I can't read my handwriting. Who are you? Two down for me. Why tell me? All the way over there. I'm going to see my paperwork. How does it work? How do I know it works? How can I tell it's working? I don't know. It does the same one. Okay, throw it away. Why hasn't my doctor told me? Why now? Can you push some of those down? I'm going to throw a wrench in there. After how does it work? See the one that says how does it work? Can you push the next three down and post a note? Yep. And write beneath how does it work? There was one about how long does it take to work? Yep. Something like that. Can you put that one up there and always before it works? There you go. How does it work and how long before it works? I think that's a problem. I'm impressed. Those should be part of every copyboard. I don't think it's contribution yet.
